questions.question: A hospital reports an outbreak of bacteremia among immunocompromised patients. Antibiotic resistant strains of Pseudomonas aeruginosa can form biofilms on indwelling medical devices, with biofilm-associated cells showing up to 1000-fold increased tolerance to disinfectants compared to planktonic cells. In recent surveillance, these strains accounted for approximately 15% of all Gram-negative isolates in intensive care units. Blood cultures from 20 patients reveal: 8 isolates are Gram-positive cocci resistant to vancomycin and ampicillin, 6 isolates are Gram-negative rods resistant to carbapenems and aminoglycosides, and 6 isolates are Gram-positive cocci sensitive to vancomycin but resistant to ampicillin. E. faecalis isolates from previous hospital outbreaks have demonstrated the ability to survive on dry surfaces for up to 7 days. In some cases, these strains also showed increased biofilm formation when exposed to sub-inhibitory concentrations of disinfectants. All Gram-negative isolates produce a green pigment on cetrimide agar and are oxidase positive. MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection has been associated with a 40% increase in ICU length of stay among immunocompromised patients. In some studies, these infections demonstrate upregulation of efflux pumps, contributing to multidrug resistance. Which bacterial species is most likely responsible for the majority of drug-resistant bacteremia cases in this outbreak?
